attractions.name: Battery Park
attractions.description: Battery Park is a historic 14-acre public park located on a bluff overlooking Lake Champlain and the Adirondack Mountains. Originally established as a military camp during the War of 1812, it now serves as a scenic community space featuring a bandshell, playground, and various monuments. The park is renowned locally for offering some of the finest sunset views in the world. (Wikipedia), HelloBurlington)

attractions.name: Burlington City Hall
attractions.description: Burlington City Hall is a historic municipal building designed by W.M. Kendall of the renowned firm McKim, Mead & White and completed in 1928. It serves as the civic heart of the city, housing the Mayor's office and various city departments while anchoring the southern end of the vibrant Church Street Marketplace. The building is a notable example of Neoclassical architecture, constructed with materials sourced primarily from Vermont, including brick, marble, and granite (Buildings of New England).

attractions.name: First Unitarian Universalist Society of Burlington
attractions.description: The First Unitarian Universalist Society of Burlington is housed in the historic Brick Meeting House, the oldest surviving place of worship in Burlington. This vibrant congregation is committed to spiritual growth, social justice, and inclusion, serving as a prominent landmark at the head of the Church Street Marketplace. The iconic building, completed in 1816, features a 170-foot steeple and has been a central hub for community and spiritual life for over two centuries.

attractions.name: Lake Champlain Islands
attractions.description: The Lake Champlain Islands are a picturesque chain of islands and a peninsula in northern Vermont, spanning roughly 30 miles between mainland Vermont and New York. This bucolic region includes the towns of South Hero, Grand Isle, North Hero, Isle La Motte, and Alburgh, offering over 200 miles of shoreline, rolling farmland, and historic sites. It is a popular destination for outdoor enthusiasts, featuring world-class biking, boating, and the oldest known fossil reef in the world. Vermont Tourism, Hello Burlington

attractions.admission: Grand Isle & Charlotte Routes: Driver & Vehicle (<19'): $12.25, Adult Passenger: $5.50, Senior (65+): $5.00, Child (6-12): $3.25, Child (under 6): FREE. All rates are each way and subject to fuel surcharges. Source: Ferries.com Rates

attractions.name: Leunig's Bistro & Café
attractions.description: Leunig's Bistro & Café is one of the oldest continually operating restaurants in downtown Burlington, offering a classic French bistro experience with the "Panache of Paris" (Official Website). Located in the heart of the Church Street Marketplace, it serves lunch and dinner with a focus on great food and a lively community atmosphere (Hello Burlington). The establishment is well-known for its exceptional people-watching opportunities and live music (Official Website).

attractions.name: Local Motion Trailside Center
attractions.description: The Local Motion Trailside Center is a non-profit organization located on the Burlington Waterfront that serves as a hub for active transportation in Vermont. It offers a wide range of bike rentals, including e-bikes and trailers, with all proceeds supporting advocacy for safer streets and better infrastructure. Visitors can access trail maps, receive expert advice, and begin their journey along the scenic 14-mile Island Line Trail. (Local Motion)
attractions.hours: Monday: 9:00 AM - 6:00 PM, Tuesday: 9:00 AM - 6:00 PM, Wednesday: 9:00 AM - 6:00 PM, Thursday: 9:00 AM - 6:00 PM, Friday: 9:00 AM - 6:00 PM, Saturday: 9:00 AM - 6:00 PM, Sunday: 9:00 AM - 6:00 PM (Tripadvisor)

attractions.admission: Up to 3 Hours: Conventional Bike $28, Conventional + Trailer $46, E-Bike $42, Kids Bike $17; All Day: Conventional Bike $42, Conventional + Trailer $65, E-Bike $74, Kids Bike $25; Additional Days: Conventional $42, E-Bike $74, Kids $25. Rental proceeds support non-profit advocacy. (Local Motion)
highlights: ['Direct access to the Burlington Bike Path and the scenic Island Line Trail.', "Wide variety of rentals including conventional bikes, e-bikes, adaptive bikes, and kids' equipment.", 'Rental proceeds directly support statewide advocacy for walking and biking safety.', 'Complimentary helmets, locks, and handlebar bags provided with every rental.', 'Information hub providing trail maps and expert local advice for cyclists and walkers.', 'Connection point for the famous Island Line Bike Ferry to the Champlain Islands.']
attractions.tips: Reservations are strongly recommended for all rentals, especially during peak weekends and sunny days. Note that e-bike rentals do not accept cash payments. Be aware that there is no after-hours drop-off, so all equipment must be returned by the 6:00 PM closing time. Allow at least 4 hours if you plan to bike the full path to the Colchester Causeway and back. (Local Motion)

attractions.name: Lone Rock Point (Rock Point Commons)
attractions.description: Lone Rock Point is a prominent natural area and promontory on the eastern shore of Lake Champlain, managed as part of the 130-acre Rock Point Commons. It is world-renowned among geologists for the Champlain Thrust Fault, where 500-million-year-old dolomite sits atop younger shale. The site offers spectacular lakeside views, easy hiking trails, and unique cliff habitats within walking distance of downtown Burlington. Wikipedia, Rock Point Commons
attractions.address: 20 Rock Point Road, Burlington, VT 05408 Rock Point Commons
attractions.hours: Dawn to Dusk Rock Point Commons
attractions.admission: Free (Donations accepted; free online trail pass highly encouraged) Rock Point Commons
highlights: 'Spectacular views of Lake Champlain and the Adirondack Mountains [Rock Point Commons', 'World-famous Champlain Thrust Fault geological formation Wikipedia', 'Over two miles of accessible walking and hiking trails Rock Point Commons', 'Unique rare and fragile plant species and cliff habitats Rock Point Commons', 'Outdoor chapel and community garden areas Tripadvisor', 'Rock climbing opportunities for advanced climbers (registration required) Rock Point Commons']
attractions.tips: Visitors are highly encouraged to obtain a free trail pass online before arriving. Parking on-site is extremely limited; it is often better to park at nearby Leddy Park or North Beach and access via the Burlington Bike Path. Dogs must be leashed at all times, and visitors should stay on marked trails to protect the fragile ecosystem. Rock Point Commons
attractions.nearby: North Beach Park, Leddy Park, Burlington Bike Path, Waterfront Park Tripadvisor

Visitors can enjoy tasting flights, expertly crafted cocktails, and a selection of local spirits, barware, and cocktail accessories in a hip, modern setting.

attractions.name: Monarch & the Milkweed
attractions.category: Restaurant, Bar, and Pastry Shop (Monarch and the Milkweed, The Food Lens)
attractions.description: Monarch & the Milkweed was a celebrated "fine diner," pastry shop, and cocktail bar located in the heart of downtown Burlington, Vermont. Known for its sophisticated marble-clad interior and artistic presentation, the establishment offered a unique blend of high-end pastries, craft coffee, and inventive cocktails. While the restaurant formally closed its dining operations in late 2022, it remains well-known as the birthplace of MILKWEED Cannabis Confections, which continues to operate through online sales and local partnerships. (Monarch and the Milkweed, Seven Days)

attractions.name: Mount Mansfield
attractions.description: Mount Mansfield is the highest mountain in Vermont, reaching an elevation of 4,393 feet and serving as a premier destination for hiking and skiing. It is renowned for its distinctive ridgeline which resembles a human profile when viewed from the east or west, with features named the Forehead, Nose, Lips, and Chin. The summit is home to one of Vermont's rare alpine tundra zones, offering stunning panoramic views of Lake Champlain and the surrounding Green Mountains. Wikipedia, Stowe Mountain Resort
attractions.hours: Auto Toll Road: 9:30 AM - 4:30 PM; Gondola SkyRide: 10:00 AM - 4:30 PM. Stowe Mountain Resort
attractions.days_open: Daily (Late May through mid-October) Stowe Mountain Resort
attractions.admission: Auto Toll Road: $38 per car/driver ($45 foliage rate), $14.50 per additional passenger ($17 foliage rate). Gondola SkyRide: $44 adult ($55 foliage rate), $29 child ages 5-12 ($35 foliage rate), Family Pack $124 ($155 foliage rate), children 4 and under are free. Stowe Mountain Resort, Gondola Pricing
highlights: ["Vermont's highest peak at 4,393 feet elevation", 'Rare and fragile alpine tundra zone at the summit ridgeline', 'Scenic 4.5-mile Auto Toll Road for easy vehicle access to the ridge', 'Gondola SkyRide offering spectacular views of the Green Mountains', "Access to the historic Long Trail, America's oldest long-distance footpath", "Distinctive 'man in the mountain' profile features including 'The Chin' summit"]
attractions.tips: Stay strictly on the rocks when in the alpine zone to protect the fragile and endangered vegetation. Be prepared for significantly cooler temperatures and high winds at the summit compared to the base. Arrive early during peak foliage season (late September to mid-October) as the Toll Road and Gondola can reach capacity quickly. Dogs must be kept on a leash at all times. Green Mountain Club, Outbound Hotels

attractions.name: Nectar's Restaurant, Bar and Music Venue
attractions.description: Nectar's is a legendary landmark in downtown Burlington, famously serving as the "birthplace" where the band Phish learned to jam. Established in 1975, it has been a cornerstone of the Vermont music scene for half a century, known for its intimate atmosphere and signature gravy fries. While the physical venue at 188 Main Street officially closed its doors in late 2025, it remains a revered site of musical pilgrimage in the city (Nectar's, VTDigger).
attractions.hours: Tuesday - Wednesday: 7:00 PM – 1:30 AM; Thursday - Saturday: 8:00 PM – 1:30 AM; Sunday - Monday: Closed (Nectar's)
attractions.admission: Varies by event; historically included many free local showcases, with ticketed touring acts typically ranging from $10 to $45+ (Nectar's, Vivid Seats)
highlights: ['Famed as the primary training ground and early home for the band Phish.', 'Features an iconic rotating neon sign that has stood over Main Street since 1975.', 'Home of the world-famous hand-cut gravy fries, a Burlington culinary staple.', '"Fresh Music Served Daily" motto reflecting its history of hosting acts every night.', 'Historic two-story layout featuring a main stage and "The Lounge" upstairs.', "Recipient of deep local support and a key contributor to the soul of Burlington's music scene."]
attractions.tips: Try the signature gravy fries, which are widely considered a must-have local delicacy. Be aware of age restrictions, as midweek shows were typically 18+ while weekend events were 21+. Check the official website for any archival updates or "Nectar's Presents" events held at other local venues (Nectar's, Seven Days).

It offers a unique urban-rural blend with extensive recreational facilities, including a popular campground, playground, and direct access to the 8-mile Burlington Greenway. Burlington Parks, Recreation & Waterfront, Hello Burlington

attractions.name: Old Mill (Main College Building)
attractions.description: Old Mill is the oldest and most iconic building on the University of Vermont campus, serving as a primary symbol of the institution for over two centuries. Originally constructed in 1825 and later modernized in the Victorian Gothic style, it now houses various humanities departments including English, Economics, and Political Science. The building's distinctive red brick facade and belfry are prominent features of the University Green Historic District.
attractions.hours: Monday - Friday: 8:00 AM - 4:30 PM (Business Hours)
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: 'Oldest campus building, with its predecessor dating back to 1802 ([Wikipedia))', 'Cornerstone of the south wing was laid by General Lafayette in 1825 (UVM History)', 'Listed on the National Register of Historic Places since 1975 (National Park Service)', 'Features a striking Victorian Gothic tower and belfry from its 1882 restoration (Wikipedia))', 'Home to the historic John Dewey Lounge, named after the famous philosopher and UVM alumnus (UVM)']
attractions.tips: The best views for photography are from across the University Green on the west side. Be sure to look for the historic Lafayette cornerstone located on the exterior of the south wing. While primarily an academic building, the interior halls and the John Dewey Lounge are often accessible to visitors during business hours.

Known for signature dishes like biscuits with herb gravy and the "Bucket-o-Spuds," it served as a bustling community hub on the corner of Cherry Street and South Winooski Avenue until its closure in late 2022. attractions.name: Shelburne Farms
attractions.category: Working Farm, Education Nonprofit, and National Historic Landmark
attractions.description: Shelburne Farms is a 1,400-acre working farm, forest, and National Historic Landmark dedicated to education for a sustainable future. Visitors can explore a historic campus featuring a world-class cheesemaking facility, a children's farmyard, and an extensive network of trails with stunning views of Lake Champlain. The property serves as a living classroom for sustainability and hosts diverse programs for educators and families alike. Shelburne Farms

The 45-acre campus includes 39 distinct structures, such as historic houses, a lighthouse, and the 220-foot steamboat Ticonderoga, set within beautifully landscaped gardens. (Shelburne Museum)

attractions.name: Stowe Village Historic District
attractions.description: The Stowe Village Historic District encompasses most of the village center of Stowe, Vermont, and has served as a major resort center since the 19th century. The district is characterized by a diverse streetscape of 19th-century wood-frame architecture and was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1978. It serves as a hub for local shopping, dining, and outdoor recreation access. (Wikipedia)

attractions.tips: Stop by the Visitor Information Center on Main Street for local advice and maps. The village is very walkable, making it easy to explore the historic buildings and local shops on foot. (Go Stowe)

attractions.name: The Cathedral Church of St. Paul
attractions.description: The Cathedral Church of St. Paul serves as the cathedral for the Episcopal Church in Vermont and is a prominent example of brutalist architecture in downtown Burlington. Completed in 1973 following a fire that destroyed the previous structure, the stressed concrete building features large windows with views of Lake Champlain. It is a vibrant center for worship, music through its "Cathedral Arts" program, and community outreach. (Official Website, Buildings of New England)
attractions.hours: Sunday: 10:00 AM (Holy Eucharist); Monday - Friday: 8:30 AM (Morning Prayer via Zoom); Sunday & Wednesday: 9:00 PM (Compline via Zoom). Office hours: Tuesday - Friday: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM (Official Website, Yelp)
attractions.admission: Free (Official Website)
highlights: 'Unique brutalist architecture designed by Burlington Associates and completed in 1973 ([Buildings of New England)', 'Stunning interior views of Lake Champlain and the Adirondack Mountains (Official Website)', 'Features a historic chime of eight bells cast in 1895 by the Meneely Bell Company (Official Website)', 'Home to a notable tracker action organ built in 1973 by Karl Wilhelm (Official Website)', 'Tranquil Memorial Garden on the east side of the nave, dating back to the 1970s (Official Website)', 'Regular host of "Cathedral Arts" events, including concerts and artistic exhibitions (Official Website)']
attractions.tips: Visitors are welcome to attend services regardless of religious background or attire; "all are welcome" is a core practice. Parking is free in the church lot on Sundays. Check the online calendar for special musical performances and Cathedral Arts events. (Official Website)

attractions.name: Town of Williston, Vermont

The town maintains a blend of historical charm, evidenced by its National Register Historic District, and modern development, particularly in the Taft Corners area. (Wikipedia, Town of Williston)

attractions.name: Trattoria Delia
attractions.description: Trattoria Delia has been a cornerstone of Burlington's dining scene for over 30 years, offering authentic Italian cuisine in a warm and hospitable environment. The restaurant is uniquely housed within the historic Vermont House, featuring an interior constructed from a relocated 19th-century New Hampshire sugar house and a focal Panton stone fireplace. Their menu celebrates seasonal ingredients sourced from local Vermont farms alongside high-quality imports from Italy.

Located in Montpelier, the smallest state capital in the United States, this Greek Revival masterpiece is one of the nation's oldest and best-preserved capitols, featuring original 19th-century interiors.

attractions.name: Albert L. Gutterson Fieldhouse
attractions.description: The Albert L. Gutterson Fieldhouse, affectionately known as \"The Gut,\" is a historic barrel-vaulted hockey arena on the University of Vermont campus. Opened in 1963, it serves as the home ice for the Vermont Catamounts men's and women's ice hockey teams and is the largest indoor arena in the state.
attractions.hours: Varies by event and academic schedule; varsity games typically start at 7:00 PM on Fridays or Saturdays (University of Vermont Athletics). Ticket Office: Tue-Fri, 10:00 AM - 5:00 PM during the academic year (University of Vermont Athletics).
attractions.days_open: Daily (varies by academic calendar and event schedule)
attractions.admission: Varsity Hockey: Prices vary by game, typically $13 - $40 (SeatGeek). UVM Students: Free with valid ID (University of Vermont Athletics). Youth (under 17): Often eligible for a $5 discount on individual game tickets (Stadium Journey). Public Skating: Fees apply for non-UVM members; ice rental is $240 per hour (University of Vermont Recreation).
highlights: 'Home to the University of Vermont Catamounts varsity ice hockey teams ([Wikipedia)', 'Features a distinctive 4,000-seat barrel-vaulted wood-paneled ceiling (Stadium51)', "Hosted the first-ever nationally televised women's hockey game in 1997 (University of Vermont Athletics)", "Served as the preseason training home for the NHL's New York Rangers from 1995 to 2004 (University of Vermont Athletics)", "Named after Albert Gutterson, the University's first Olympic gold medalist (Wikipedia)", 'Largest indoor arena in the state of Vermont (Ice Hockey Wiki)']
attractions.tips: Arrive early for hockey games as sell-outs are common and the atmosphere is intense. Parking is available in the adjacent Patrick Gymnasium/Gutterson Parking Garage, though it can fill quickly during major events. The arena is kept quite cold for the ice, so spectators are advised to dress warmly even during winter months. Always check the official UVM EMS master schedule for up-to-date public skating and open recreation times.

Originally the University of Vermont's main library, it now houses the Silver Special Collections Library, the Miller Center for Holocaust Studies, and other academic centers. The building is a centerpiece of the University Green Historic District and is listed on the National Register of Historic Places (Wikipedia, UVM History).

attractions.name: Marquis de Lafayette Statue (University of Vermont)
attractions.description: The Marquis de Lafayette Statue is a monumental bronze sculpture dedicated in 1883 to commemorate the French general's 1825 visit to the University of Vermont, where he laid the cornerstone for the 'Old Mill' building. Created by renowned American sculptor John Quincy Adams Ward, the seven-foot-tall statue depicts Lafayette in 18th-century civilian dress, standing on a rusticated Quincy granite pedestal. Originally centered on the University Green, it was relocated to the north end in 1921 to accommodate the Ira Allen Statue and remains a central symbol of the university's historical connection to the American Revolutionary hero.
attractions.address: University Green (near University Place and Colchester Avenue), Burlington, VT 05405
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours (Outdoor public space)
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: ['Created by prominent American sculptor John Quincy Adams Ward and dedicated in 1883.', "Commemorates General Lafayette's 1825 visit to UVM to lay the cornerstone of the Old Mill building.", 'The statue stands over seven feet tall and is cast in bronze in Philadelphia.', 'Sited on a granite pedestal designed to overlook the campus green.', 'Successfully restored in 2013, including the replacement of its historically missing cane.', 'Located on the historic University Green, a central park-like setting for campus life and student expression.']
attractions.tips: The statue is best viewed during daylight hours to appreciate the bronze detail and its position on the University Green. Since it is located on an active university campus, visitors should check for any local parking restrictions or event closures on the UVM website. It is a popular spot for photos, especially given its proximity to the historic Old Mill and Ira Allen Chapel.

Constructed between 1904 and 1907, it was named after U.S. Senator Justin Smith Morrill, the author of the Land Grant College Act. The building is a contributing property to the University Green Historic District and remains a central hub for agricultural education and extension services in Vermont. UVM History, UVM Events

attractions.name: Williams Hall (University of Vermont)

attractions.name: Burlington Greenway (Burlington Bike Path)
attractions.description: The Burlington Greenway is a scenic, 8-mile paved multi-use path that runs along the shore of Lake Champlain from south to north. It connects residential neighborhoods with downtown Burlington and several major parks, serving as a popular route for commuters, runners, and cyclists year-round (City of Burlington).
attractions.address: 645 Pine Street, Suite B (Parks & Recreation Office)
attractions.hours: Monday - Sunday: Dawn to Dusk (Burlington Parks, Recreation & Waterfront)
attractions.admission: Free (City of Burlington)
highlights: '8-mile paved path with spectacular views of Lake Champlain and the Adirondack Mountains ([Hello Burlington)', 'Direct connection to major parks including Oakledge Park, Waterfront Park, North Beach, and Leddy Park (Vermont Explored)', 'Part of the larger 14-mile Island Line Trail that extends onto the historic Colchester Causeway (Wikipedia)', 'Seasonal access to the Bike Ferry, which connects the trail to the Champlain Islands (Local Motion)', 'Plowed and maintained during winter for year-round recreational use (City of Burlington)', 'Features fitness stations and seasonal public restrooms along the route (City of Burlington)']
attractions.tips: Pedestrians have the right-of-way; cyclists must yield and alert others when passing. During summer, bring a swimsuit to enjoy the public beaches at North Beach or Oakledge Park. Bike rentals are available downtown at Local Motion and North Star Sports (City of Burlington).
attractions.nearby: ECHO Leahy Center for Lake Champlain, Church Street Marketplace, Oakledge Park Earth Clock, North Beach Campground, Leddy Park Arena (Vermont Explored)

attractions.description: Deep City is a brunch restaurant and bar located in a historic 1800s lumber mill on the shores of Lake Champlain. In 2024, the restaurant partnered with Charles Reeves, founder of the beloved Penny Cluse Café, to bring back popular brunch staples to the Burlington waterfront. The space features a modern industrial design with exposed brick and beams, offering both indoor seating and a scenic patio. Official Site

The center features over 100 interactive exhibits and 70 species of live animals, focusing on the ecology and stewardship of the Lake Champlain Basin. Operating under the motto "Destroy Apathy," it combined a New American restaurant and craft distillery with a sprawling performance venue and art gallery. The space was renowned for hosting diverse cultural events, including live music, storytelling slams, and the popular seasonal "Truck Stop" food truck block party (Vermont.com, Seven Days).

attractions.name: Magic Hat Brewing Company and Performing Arts Center
attractions.description: Magic Hat Brewing Company is a legendary craft brewery founded in 1994, famous for its flagship #9 "not quite pale ale" and its whimsical, art-focused branding. The company's former headquarters, known as the Artifactory, served as a "zany" performance space and brewery destination that helped cement the Burlington area as a premier beer mecca. Although production moved to New York in 2020, the brand remains an iconic part of Vermont's craft brewing history. (Seven Days, American Craft Beer)

(Art Map Burlington)', 'Pilot Batch Offerings: Exclusive, small-batch experimental beers that were only available on-tap at the brewery. (Tripadvisor)', "Mardi Gras Celebration: The brewery was the longtime sponsor of Burlington's massive annual Mardi Gras parade. (Vermont Biz)"]

attractions.name: Oakledge Park
attractions.description: Oakledge Park is a scenic lakeside retreat situated along the picturesque shores of Lake Champlain in Burlington's South End. This local favorite features both rocky and sandy beaches, expansive lawns, and the world’s first universally accessible public community treehouse. Visitors can enjoy stunning waterfront views of the Adirondack Mountains while utilizing the park's extensive sports facilities and picnic areas (Hello Burlington, Vermonter).
attractions.hours: Monday - Friday: 7:00 AM - Sunset; Saturday - Sunday: 1 hour before sunrise - Sunset (Vermonter)
attractions.admission: Free for entry; parking is $3 per hour from May through October 31 and free from November through May (City of Burlington). Shelter rentals are available for half-day ($190) or full-day ($275) periods (Hello Burlington).
highlights: 'Burlington Earth Clock: A massive granite sundial and calendar located along the waterfront path ([Atlas Obscura).', 'Universally Accessible Playground: An inclusive playspace designed for children of all abilities (Oakledge For All).', 'Two Public Beaches: Access to both Blanchard Beach and Oakledge Cove for swimming and kayaking (Vermont.com).', 'Burlington Greenway: Direct access to the 8-mile paved multi-use bike path that runs through the heart of the park (Trail Finder).', 'Sports Courts: Facilities for tennis, volleyball, bocce, and an active kickball league (City of Burlington).', "Accessible Treehouse: The world's first public universally accessible community treehouse (The Treehouse Guys)."]
attractions.tips: Parking is paid via kiosks or the ParkMobile app (Zone 5818) during peak months. For the best experience, visit at sunset to see the sun dip behind the Adirondacks, and keep dogs on a leash at all times (City of Burlington, Tripadvisor).
attractions.nearby: Red Rocks Park, Pine Street Arts District, World's Largest Filing Cabinet, Burlington Waterfront Park, Echo Leahy Center for Lake Champlain (Vermont Condo Pros, Yelp).

The site functions as a cultural center that interprets the history of the Winooski Intervale, including the lives of its indigenous inhabitants and frontier settlers (Official Site, Wikipedia).
